texts.orig: Steady-state permeability  coefficients have been measured for equimolar mixtures of C02-CzH4, COZ-C~H~, and CzH4-C3H8,  as well as for a mixture of 74.9 mol % C02 and 25.1 mol% C2H4 in polyethylene membranes.  The measurements were made at 20,35, and 50°C and at  pressures of up to 28 atm. Each component of the permeating mixtures studied had the effect of increasing the permeability coefficient for the other component. Furthermore, at equal partial pressures and at  the same temperature, the component exhibiting the highest solubility in the polymer had the largest effect  in  increasing  the  permeability  coefficient  of  the other  component. This behavior  is  in agreement with the predictions of a free-volume model for the permeation of gas mixtures proposed by Fang, Stern, and Frisch. From a quantitative viewpoint, the permeability coefficients for the components of the mixtures agreed, on the average, to better than 25% with the predicted values. The theoretical permeability coefficients can be estimated from the model by using parameters determined with the pure components only.

texts.orig: The development of new membrane processes for the separation of gases  would be substantially facilitated by a better understanding of the mechanism of permeation of gas mixtures through nonporous polymer membranes. Very little work has been done in this area compared with the many studies reported in the literature on the permeation of pure gases.  Stern,  Fang, and Frischl have proposed a 'free-volume''  model  of gas permeation which was later extended by these investigators to the permeation of mixtures.2 The model is based on Fujita's3 free-volume theory of diffusion of small molecules in polymers, and is thus applicable at  temperatures above the glass  transition temperature of the  polymers. Recent steady-state permeation rate studies and non-steady-state absorption studies4y5 with a number of simple penetrant gases, such as C02, CH4, C2H4, and C3H8, in polyethylene have provided support for the free-volume model of gas permeation.' Also, an extension of the model to mixtures2  was found to describe satisfactorily the permeation  of N20-CO2 and C ~ H ~ - C ~ H I ~ mixtures through polyethylene. texts.orig: The objective of the present study was to test the validity of the free-volume model of  permeation  of gas mixtures2 over a wider range of  pressures and at several temperatures. Accordingly, rates of permeation of four binary mixtures of COz, C2H4, and C3Hs through polyethylene membranes were measured at 20, 35,  and 5OoC  and at  several  pressures up to 28  atm. The permeability coefficients for the components of these mixtures, determined from permeation rates, were compared with values calculated from the free-volume model for the experimental conditions. Parameters required by the model fcr this purpose were obtained from permeability measurements with the pure components of the gas mixtures studied.
texts.text: The objective of the present study was to test the validity of the free-volume model of  permeation  of gas mixtures2 over a wider range of  pressures and at several temperatures. Accordingly, rates of permeation of four binary mixtures of COz, C2H4, and C3Hs through polyethylene membranes were measured at 20, 35,  and 5OoC  and at  several  pressures up to 28  atm. The permeability coefficients for the components of these mixtures, determined from permeation rates, were compared with values calculated from the free-volume model for the experimental conditions. Parameters required by the model fcr this purpose were obtained from permeability measurements with the pure components of the gas mixtures studied.

texts.orig: The separation of a gas mixture by selective permeation through a nonporous polymer membrane is illustrated in Figure 1,  which shows a diagram of a single permeation stage such as the permeability apparatus used in the present study. The stage is divided into two compartments by the polymer membrane. A feed stream, in this case a binary gas mixture of components a and b, is introduced in the lower compartment of the stage at  some suitable molar flow rate Li(h) and constant total pressure Ph. A fraction of the feed is allowed to permeate through the membrane into the upper compartment of the stage, which is maintained at a constant total pressure pl  ( < p h ) . The membrane is assumed to be more permeable to component a than to component b. The feed, containing x? mole fraction of component a, is thus divided into two product streams: (i) a permeated (low-pressure)  stream enriched in component a, and (ii)  an unpermeated (high-pressure)  stream depleted in that component. The flow rates of the two product streams leaving the stage are L,(l) and Lo(h) and the mole fractions of a in these streams are y:  and x: , respectively. The fraction of the feed allowed to permeate through the membrane, 8 = Lo(l)/Li(h), is known as the 'stage cut.' The compositions of the product streams, for a given membrane area, depend on a number of factors: (i) the feed composition, (ii)  the nature of the polymer

texts.orig: The simplest flow pattern in a permeation stage,  which was used in the present study, is that of  "perfect mixing." In this case, the composition of the unpermeated gas mixture in the high-pressure compartment of the stage is uniform and the same as that of the unpermeated stream leaving the stage. Similarly, the composition of the permeated gas mixture in the low-pressure compartment is uniform and identical with that of  the permeated stream leaving the stage. Hence, the composition of the feed stream changes as a step function at the stage inlet. texts.orig: Permeability coefficients  for the components of a gas mixture were determined with valves 1,2,3,6,10, and 12  open, and valves 4,5,7,8, and 9 closed.  The gas mixture from a high-pressure cylinder was supplied to the permeability cell via valve 1  at  a desired total pressure which was measured with gauge F.  The mixture flowed continuously through the upper (high-pressure)  section of the permeability cell and was discharged to the atmosphere via valves 3 and 10  and an oil trap (or 'bubbler') kl. A fraction of the feed mixture (the  'stage cut') was allowed to permeate through the membrane into the lower (low-pressure)  section of the cell,  and was continuously discharged to  the atmosphere via valves 6 and 12 and oil trap k2. The oil traps prevented a back-diffusion of  air into the permeability cell. The total pressure pl of the permeated gas was constant and could be measured by means of an open-end mercury manometer I.  The stage cut could be adjusted by means of valve 2, which was a micrometering  valve.  The stage cut was determined from the flow rates of the high- and low-pressure gas streams.  The former was measured by means of a rotameter G, while the latter was measured with the borosilicate capillary H as described above for pure-gas permeation. The high-pressure gas could be diverted into the rotameter by opening valve 4 and closing valve 3. The low-pressure stream was allowed to enter the borosilicate capillary by opening valve 5 and closing valve 6.

texts.orig: It should be noted that the composition of the above two streams could be markedly affected by their flow patterns in the permeability Consequently, these flow patterns had to be well defined. In the present study, the cell was operated under 'perfect mixing' conditions because of the mathematical simplicity o f   the model describing  this flow pattern.8  In order to obtain a 'perfectly mixed' permeability cell, it is necessary that the compositions of the high- and low-pressure streams at all points in the cell be the same as the compositions of  the corresponding product  streams leaving the cell. This condition  was simulated by performing the measurements at  low stage cuts (<0.15) only.  The composition  of  the high-pressure stream  then  changed  very  little along the membrane, as would be the case in a thoroughly  mixed stream. At the same time, the low-pressure stream was well mixed by providing a sufficiently large mixing volume, since the flow rate of this stream was very small.

texts.orig: The membranes were cut from a polyethylene  sheet supplied by Union Carbide Corp. The commercial-type polyethylene was custom-made on a tubular film extruder and contained no additives. The density of the polyethylene was 0.918 g/cm3, corresponding to an amorphous volume fraction of 0.55. The melt index of the polymer w a s reported to be 2.0. The nominal thickness of the polyethylene sheet was 5.08 X cm (2 X in.). Two membrane samples, with an effective permeation  area of  7.92 cm2 (1.23 in.2) each, were used in the present study. Permeability coefficients determined with the two membrane samples and a given pure penetrant agreed within 2% for all gases studied.

However, the activation energy for permeation of propane was found to be 4.76 kcal/g mol at 5.25 atm,  which is considerably smaller than obtained by Fang14  (9.13  kcal/g rnol at 5 atm) and Michaels and Bixler15  (11.2 kcal/g mol at 1  atm).  Although the activation energy for propane is expected to be a function of  pressure (the value of Michaels and Bixler is for a lower pressure),  the deviation from the literature values is probably due to the high uncertainty arising from the fact that the activation energy in the present work was determined from permeability measurements at only two temperatures.

texts.orig: Figures 6-13 show that, for all the mixtures investigated, the experimental permeability  coefficients for the components of  the permeating mixtures are higher than the permeability coefficients for the pure components. In other words, each component of a mixture enhances the rate of transport of the  other component, as predicted  by the free-volume model  (since y i > 0 for all the components). The agreement between the experimental and theoretical permeability coefficients varies from 5%  to  a maximum of about 40%, the latter in the case of the 74.9% COr25.1%  C2H4 mixture at 2OOC. However,  in most cases this agreement is better than 25%.

texts.orig: It has been reported that the free-volume model also describes satisfactorily the permeation of C02-N20 mixtures through polyethylene at 28 and 41 atm and at 30°C,2 as well as that of C~H~-C~H~C, mixtures through polyethylene at 1  atm and 30°C.2,6 Furthermore, the model appears to be applicable to  the permeation of  certain  liquid  mixtures, such as benzene-n-hexane2J8  and benzene-cyclohexane2.19; however, in view of the relatively high solubility of the components of these mixtures in polyethylene,  any agreement between theory and experiment may well be fortuitous. The assumption of additivity of the y'ui terms in eq. (9) restricts the applicability of  the free-volume model to low penetrant concentrations, probably of less than 0.2 total volume fraction of penetrant. This assumption implies that the components of permeating mixtures do  not interact with each other: each component affects the rate of transport  of  the other components only indirectly, by  increasing the fractional free volume  of  the penetrant mixture-polymer  system. The interactions between all components will have to be taken into  account when extending the model to  the permeation of more highly soluble fluid mixtures, which may plasticize the polymer.
texts.text: It has been reported that the free-volume model also describes satisfactorily the permeation of C02-N20 mixtures through polyethylene at 28 and 41 atm and at 30°C,2 as well as that of C~H~-C~H~C, mixtures through polyethylene at 1  atm and 30°C.2,6 Furthermore, the model appears to be applicable to  the permeation of  certain  liquid  mixtures, such as benzene-n-hexane2J8  and benzene-cyclohexane2.19; however, in view of the relatively high solubility of the components of these mixtures in polyethylene,  any agreement between theory and experiment may well be fortuitous. The assumption of additivity of the y'ui terms in eq. (9) restricts the applicability of  the free-volume model to low penetrant concentrations, probably of less than 0.2 total volume fraction of penetrant. This assumption implies that the components of permeating mixtures do  not interact with each other: each component affects the rate of transport  of  the other components only indirectly, by  increasing the fractional free volume  of  the penetrant mixture-polymer  system. The interactions between all components will have to be taken into  account when extending the model to  the permeation of more highly soluble fluid mixtures, which may plasticize the polymer.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is interesting that the permeation behavior of gas mixtures described above for 'rubbery' polyethylene is just the opposite of that observed with 'glassy' polymers, where each component decreases the rate of permeation of the other components owing to competition for fixed sites.20  The latter behavior could occur also above the glass transition temperature of a polymer, i.e., in the rubbery polymer state, if the values of y of all the components of the permeating mixture were negative.

texts.orig: Finally, it should be noted that correlations have been developed for the parameters Ad, &, and y,*  as well as for solubility coefficients21  of pure gases in polyethylene. These correlations may be used, in conjunction with the freevolume model, to predict permeability coefficients for the components of gas mixtures at  various pressure and temperatures. Considerable further work with other gas mixtures and polymers is required before the validity of the free-volume model can be fully ascertained.
